title: Paired Muscle Relaxation, Step by Step If you have decided to practice paired muscle relaxation, it can be very helpful to practice relaxing each of your muscles first.

pages.text:
331
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission

Distress Tolerance Handout 6b   (Distress Tolerance Worksheet 4a)
Paired Muscle Relaxation, Step by Step
If you have decided to practice paired muscle relaxation, it can be very helpful to practice
relaxing each of your muscles first.
When you are starting, practice in a quiet place to reduce distractions, and make sure that you
have enough time. As you improve with practice, you will want to practice in many different kinds of
places, so that you can relax effectively when you most need to.
Remember that effectiveness improves with practice. If judgments arise, observe them, let
them go, and return to your practice. If you become anxious, try focusing on breathing in to the
count of 5 and out to the count of 7 (or the counts you have already determined for paced breathing),
breathing all the while into your belly until you can return to relaxation exercises.
Now that you are ready to begin .ย .ย .
1.	 Get your body into a comfortable position in which you can relax. Loosen tight clothing. Lie or
sit down, with all body parts uncrossed and no body part supporting any others.
2.	 For each area of the body listed below, gather tension by tightening muscles. Focus on the
sensation of tightness in and around that area. Hold the tension as you inhale for 5โ6 seconds,
then release and breathe out.
3.	 As you release, say in your mind very slowly the word โRelax.โ
4.	 Observe the changes in sensations as you relax for 10โ15 seconds then move on to the next
muscle.
Start first with each of the 16 muscle groups.
Once you can do that, practice with medium groups of muscles and then large groups.
Once you are good at that, practice tensing your entire body at once.
When you tense your entire body, you are like a robotโstiff, nothing moving.
When you relax your entire body, you are like a rag dollโall muscles drooping down.
Once you can relax all your muscles, practice three or four times a day until you can routinely
relax your entire body rapidly.
By practicing pairing exhaling and the word โRelaxโ with relaxing your muscles, you will
eventually be able to relax just by letting go and saying the word โRelax.โ
Large
Medium
Small
๏ฃฑ๏ฃด๏ฃฒ๏ฃด ๏ฃณ
๏ฃฑ
๏ฃฒ
๏ฃณ
  1.	 Hands and wrists: Make fists with both hands and pull fists up on the wrists.
  2.	Lower and upper arms: Make fists and bend both arms up to touch your shoulders.
  3.	Shoulders: Pull both shoulders up to your ears.
๏ฃฑ
๏ฃด
๏ฃด
๏ฃฒ
๏ฃด
๏ฃด
๏ฃณ
๏ฃฑ๏ฃด๏ฃฒ๏ฃด ๏ฃณ
  4.	 Forehead: Pull eyebrows close together, wrinkling forehead.
  5.	 Eyes: Shut eyes tightly.
  6.	Nose and upper cheeks: Scrunch up nose; bring upper lips and cheeks up toward eyes.
๏ฃฑ
๏ฃฒ
๏ฃณ
  7.	 Lips and lower face: Press lips together; bring edges of lips back toward ears.
  8.	Tongue and mouth: Teeth together; tongue pushing on upper mouth.
๏ฃฑ๏ฃด๏ฃฒ๏ฃด ๏ฃณ
  9.	 Neck: Push head back into chair, floor, or bed, or push chin down to chest.
๏ฃฑ
๏ฃฒ
๏ฃณ
10.	 Chest: Take deep breath and hold it.
11.	 Back: Arch back, bringing shoulder blades together.
๏ฃฑ
๏ฃด
๏ฃด
๏ฃฒ
๏ฃด
๏ฃด
๏ฃณ
๏ฃฑ
๏ฃฒ
๏ฃณ
12.	 Stomach: Hold stomach in tightly.
13.	 Buttocks: Squeeze buttocks together.
๏ฃฑ๏ฃด๏ฃฒ๏ฃด ๏ฃณ
14.	 Upper legs and thighs: Legs out; tense thighs.
15.	 Calves: Legs out; point toes down.
16.	 Ankles: Legs out; point toes together, heels out, toes curled under.
Remember, paired relaxation is a skill. It takes time to develop. With practice, you will notice the benefits.
Note. Adapted from Smith, R. E. (1980). Development of an integrated coping response through cognitiveโaffective stress management train-
ing. In I. G. Sarason & C. D. Spielberger (Eds.), Stress and anxiety (Vol. 7, pp.ย 265โ280). Washington, DC: Hemisphere. Copyright 1980 by
Hemisphere Publishing Corporation. Adapted by permission.

Distress Tolerance Handout 6b   (Distress Tolerance Worksheet 4a)
Paired Muscle Relaxation, Step by Step
If you have decided to practice paired muscle relaxation, it can be very helpful to practice
relaxing each of your muscles first.
When you are starting, practice in a quiet place to reduce distractions, and make sure that you

Now that you are ready to begin .ย .ย .

sit down, with all body parts uncrossed and no body part supporting any others.

Start first with each of the 16 muscle groups.
Once you can do that, practice with medium groups of muscles and then large groups.
Once you are good at that, practice tensing your entire body at once.
When you tense your entire body, you are like a robotโstiff, nothing moving.
When you relax your entire body, you are like a rag dollโall muscles drooping down.

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Paired Muscle Relaxation, Step by Step If you have decided to practice paired muscle relaxation, it can be very helpful to practice relaxing each of your muscles first.
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Distress tolerance means getting through the wave without adding harm. Use gentle, medically safer versions first.
tiny_steps: Choose one body-safe skill for 30 seconds.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Replace intense exercise with hand squeeze, paced exhale, safe fidget, weighted comfort if tolerated, or visual grounding.
Avoid cold shock/breath-holding/intense exercise unless already clinician-approved and safe that day.
Keep oxygen, rescue meds, allergy plan, mobility aids, and exit routes accessible.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not use crisis skills as punishment, sensory flooding, or exposure without consent.
